source.title: Common Neural Network for Different Functions: An Investigation of Proactive and Reactive Inhibition

confidence_rationale: Human fMRI with dynamic causal modelling showing distinct fronto-basal ganglia pathways for proactive (prepare-to-inhibit) vs. reactive (cancel-in-flight) inhibition, with a longer DLPFC-caudate-IFG pathway for proactive and a shorter IFG-SMA-STN pathway for reactive. This maps onto REE's pre-commit vs. post-commit suppression channels and gives mechanistic grounding for why the arbitration policy needs to operate at two different timescales within the same anatomical network.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Both proactive and reactive inhibition recruit IFG, SMA, STN, and M1; proactive inhibition additionally recruits a longer DLPFC-caudate-IFG pathway that modulates downstream control; the shorter IFG-SMA-STN hyperdirect pathway mediates reactive inhibition. DCM shows that caudate-to-IFG connectivity is specifically modulated by proactive but not reactive inhibition.
mapping.ree_translation: In REE's tri-loop architecture, the proactive/reactive distinction maps onto E3-gate arbitration (plan ahead to prevent a bad action -- proactive) vs. post-commitment suppression (cancel an action already in progress -- reactive). The longer prefrontal-caudate pathway for proactive control implies that the planning loop (E3) must feed into the gate-conflict arbitration *before* commitment, not just at the moment of execution. For Q-016, this means the arbitration policy must operate at two timescales: a proactive E3-driven preparation of gate priorities (using the DLPFC-caudate path analog) and a reactive emergency-cancel mechanism (using the hyperdirect STN path analog). A policy that only implements one of these risks either chronic over-preparation (never committing) or chronic under-suppression (unable to cancel).
mapping.mapping_caveat: The stop-signal paradigm used probes inhibition of simple motor responses, not the kind of multi-loop conflict involving incommensurable error signals that Q-016 concerns. The proactive manipulation (participants prepared to inhibit a response they knew might need to be suppressed) is closer to REE's E3 pre-commit simulation than to a genuine three-loop conflict. The DCM connectivity findings are correlational evidence of pathway engagement, not direct evidence of what arbitration policy is optimal.
mapping.source_context: Human fMRI, stop-signal paradigm, dynamic causal modelling (70 models compared), healthy adult participants

confidence_components.notes: Solid human fMRI with rigorous model comparison. Transfer risk moderate -- stop-signal inhibition is a proxy for but not equivalent to tri-loop conflict arbitration. The proactive/reactive distinction is the most directly REE-relevant finding.
